div.head: 1、2號刻經窟概覽
div.p: 1、2號窟是本處遺址三組刻經雙窟中的一組。另兩組是83、85號窟和109、110號窟。 構成雙窟的二窟左右相鄰，中間共用一道隔牆。窟室的形狀、大小基本一致，雙窟底部平齊。 這三組經窟恰好都位於崖面的較高位置。在83至85號雙窟中，83號窟刊刻了部份《大般涅槃經》，85號窟內則為完整的《維摩詰所說經》。 109號窟和110號窟刻《大方便佛報恩經》，但僅刻出七卷經文前五卷，六面窟壁也只使用了五面。 1、2號窟是幾近完工的一組雙窟，其內刊刻一部完整的佛經──《妙法蓮華經》，且所有窟內壁面均佈滿刻經文字。
刻經工作的主持者決定選用當時最為流行的《妙法蓮華經》版本，即鳩摩羅什（334-413）七卷二十八品的譯本。 經文總計約71,000字，數量幾乎是臥佛院現存刻經的五分之一。

標準大小的方形窟──每壁可刊刻大約10,000字──無法為整部經文提供足夠的壁面空間。因此開窟者在標準窟的基礎上將窟室進一步加高。 正如在導論中討論到的，這些經過改造的窟室，平面與標準洞窟的相差不大。1號窟的窟口寬211釐米，2號窟為206釐米。 而三壁也僅比通常的2米略寬一些。差別主要體現在高度上。加高洞窟使得窟壁面積也隨之擴大， 幫助建造者更有效地達到他們在營建過程中的兩大工作目標：在減少鑿石量的同時盡可能地擴大壁面空間， 即以最小的工程量獲得最大的收益。1號窟d壁在窟口處高275釐米，f壁在窟口處則高282釐米。 2號窟中相應位置的測量值略小一些，分別是265釐米和260釐米。也許當開窟工程進行到2號窟時， 工匠們已經計算出無須鑿出1號窟那樣的高度便可以刻完整部經。

書丹者自1號窟的d壁開始工作，在壁面上規劃出一塊布局嚴謹、規整的文字區──不僅有邊框，還有垂直線和水平線構成的界格， 於是文字便被安排在邊長約為2.1×2.1釐米、完全等大的方格中，橫成排，縱成行。與此同時，他們還盡可能地充分利用可用空間。文字區於d壁窟口處只比打磨面窄1釐米左右。待書至e壁時，書丹者一開始仍以相同的方式繼續，但自75行之後， 他們放棄使用橫排，所書經文不再左右對齊。這種不規則便於他們在一行之内放入更多的字。 在最後的17行，他們還縮小了行距，經文布局更加緊湊。書丹者可能擔心如果按照前面的節奏繼續，壁面空間會不夠用。 出於同樣的考慮，他們也壓縮了f壁第48行之後的行距。就這樣， 書丹者設法在1號窟完成了《妙法蓮華經》卷四第十一品，《見寶塔品》之前的全部經文。

figure.head: 1號窟刻文區概覽，自右往左依次為d壁、e壁和f壁
Overview of the fields of script in cave 1, on walls d, e, and f, from right to left

div.p: 在與之毗鄰的2號窟，書丹者從第十二品開始繼續經文的書寫工作。這時他們拋棄了界格與標示文字區域的矩形邊框。 自d壁第55行起，經文之起頭呈階梯狀高出6釐米左右，這樣一行便可容納更多的刻字。 如何更好地節省空間，這是書丹者一直在考慮的問題， 以至於他們將第十七品開頭的經品名刻到了d壁最後一行底端這個不甚理想的位置，然後直接在e壁開始該品經的正文部份。 書丹者在e壁享有了更多的空間，這是因為該壁略微內凹，加之洞窟平面前窄後寬。測量數據顯示，窟口寬206釐米，e壁寬221釐米。 由於此前在對可用空間的利用上過分“吝嗇”，書丹者一定意識到，到了最後的f壁，他們可以適當地“慷慨”一些了。 於是間距為2.0至2.1釐米的垂直界格線被再次啟用，不過水平線仍未畫出。書丹者在最後一壁只用71行便完成了整部經典， 這時經文距離窟口尚有42釐米。而打磨面卻一直延伸至窟口處。倘若他們願意的話，還有足夠的壁面來寫刻《後序》，《大正藏》T#263, 9: 62b-c。 像66號窟那樣， 刻一行題記。

figure.head: 1、2號窟二窟平面圖；雷魯卡繪製
Ground plans of caves 1 and 2; drawing Lukas Ledderose

div.p: 1、2號窟六壁刻經布局的不斷變化表明，書丹者事先沒有或不能很好地計算出最終需要多大的壁面空間。 其所作改動反映出隨著工作的開展，他們對剩餘工作量的估算。由此不難想像，當時其他的窟龕開鑿者和書丹者也是如此。
書丹者使用了一套文本標記，有條不紊地標識各卷、各品經文的開頭與結束。他們用代表卷數的小數字來標明各卷開頭。 現在僅“四”、“五”、“六”三字尚清晰可見。其他數字可依1號窟f壁第48行表示第四卷開頭的小字“四”補出。
每卷的結束用經名加卷次的形式標出，例如“妙法蓮華經卷第三”。目前此類標記僅見於卷三、四、六、七。 其餘三卷的卷尾標記依1號窟f壁第48行第三卷的標記補出。
在一品的開頭，書丹者常重複書寫經名，接著為品名、品次，例如“妙法蓮華經隨喜功德品第十八”。 第一、五、九、十、十一、十二、十四、二十一和二十三各品的開頭標記現已不存，可依2號窟e壁第20行第十八品的開頭補出。
不同於卷的情況，品的結束沒有標記。

div.head: 位置

list.item: 該窟位於G區，是北岩最西端的洞窟。窟底距現今路面約7米（ ； ）。
f壁方向：148°。

div.p: 1、2號窟所居崖面位置在遺址中已屬最高，但在唐代可能還要高出幾米。此處鄰近巨型臥佛造像， 顯然是開鑿龕窟的理想位置。2號窟的d壁東距臥佛足部9米， 兩窟底部與佛足部位力士像的頭頂平齊（；）。

div.head: 形制
div.p: 1號窟是敞口方形窟，雙層窟口（）。同另外兩組雙窟一樣，1、2號窟共用一長方形外窟口（）。此外窟口僅稍作修整，不及內窟口規整， 臥佛院所有的洞窟基本如此。兩窟共有的外窟口頂並不寬大，超出1號窟內窟口約40釐米。 在窟外低於現水泥地面約45釐米的地方，外窟口底部形成了一個連通兩窟的狹窄走道，現僅餘少許殘跡。

外窟口東壁，即與d壁平行的壁面，有一大小為15×11釐米的長方形孔洞， 開鑿於窟頂下方16釐米、內窟口以外38釐米處。孔洞的保存情況良好，僅邊緣略有風化。 西壁在距窟頂13釐米、內窟口33釐米的位置上，開鑿有一個與其對應的、邊長為15釐米的方孔。這個方孔殘損嚴重，現存深度僅2至4釐米。 過去可能有一條橫木安插於兩孔之中，或為門框的上部橫樑，或用於懸掛帳幕。2號窟外窟口側壁上，則不見此類成對孔洞之痕跡。
窟室平面近方形，前窄後寬；窟頂由窟口處向後部略微下傾，大體仍為平頂。

list.item: d壁：寬205釐米，前部高275釐米，後部高262釐米。
e壁：寬254釐米，中部高268釐米。
壁：寬224釐米，前部高282釐米，後部高266釐米。
雙層窟口：內窟口寬211釐米；外窟口東、西兩側分別較內窟口寬35、22釐米，兩側殘深分別為80、70釐米左右。

div.head: 保存狀況
div.p: 1982年，曾對刻經壁面進行保護性化學封護處理。李良與鄧之金第38頁b欄。 1、2號窟之間的外立面下部崩塌不存，現用水泥修補抹平。 殘損窟口已用水泥從三面（左右兩側及頂部）加固。窟內原地面早期即遭破壞，僅在窟室西北角與東北角留存少許， 高出現今水泥地面10釐米左右。窟頂前部風化較嚴重，大體呈黃色，後部岩石呈片狀剝落。窟室後部有泥土堆積， 其上現長有低矮植物。

list.item: 打磨面的面積約為240×183釐米，基本覆蓋整個壁面， 僅在窟口處和底部留有寬度分別為23釐米與10釐米（距離現今水泥地面，而非原地平）的未打磨帶。
刻文字區面積約為205×168釐米，距窟口約24釐米、e壁13至16釐米，其上緣距窟頂20釐米，下緣距現今地面25釐米左右。

div.head: 保存狀況
div.p: 不良。有一大塊褐色壁面，自窟口距地面208釐米的地方開始向窟內延伸106釐米，表層有2至3釐米厚的壁面脫落。 其下方壁面亦有多處小塊剝落。窟頂以下約65釐米處的一塊水平區域和壁面後部、自窟口內162釐米高處開始的一條垂直， 是d壁保存最為完好的兩塊區域。

row.cell: ［妙法蓮華經序品第一］；現已不可見“序品”二字在80年代製作的拓片上仍清楚可見。見。，根據2號窟e壁第20行類推補出。 由1號窟e壁第13行以及1號窟f壁第48行的經品名寫刻情況推測，其後還有一較小的“一”字， 亦風化不得見。 表示《妙法蓮華經》第一品《序品》，同時也是該經首卷，自此開始。
［……］我聞一時佛……；《法華經》卷一第一品《序品》可辨識出的開頭文字。
……說《法華經》今相如本瑞是諸［……］盡無有餘；《法華經》卷一第一品目前可看到的最後幾字。
妙［法蓮華經方便品第二 爾時］世尊從三昧……；《法華經》卷一第二品《方便品》的開頭部份。 泯滅字跡依2號窟e壁第20行類推補出。
……爪甲,而畫作佛像［如是］諸人［等，漸］漸［積］功［德，具足大］； d壁刻經結束，至此仍屬《法華經》卷一第二品《方便品》。此行爲d壁末行。

list.item: 打磨面覆蓋整個壁面，面積約為254×258釐米，其底端距現今水泥地面約10釐米。
刻文區自窟頂下方21釐米處始，距d壁約13釐米、f壁約12釐米。下緣已風化，無法辨識。

row.cell: 僅第一部份；75行之後經文漸趨不規整，不再左右對齊
約為0.5至1.0釐米，最後17行縮小為0.3至0.7釐米
約為0.5至1.2釐米，最後17行縮小為0.3至0.7釐米

div.head: 保存狀況
div.p: 保存不良。距窟底約86釐米的壁面殘失，目前該部位由於滲水的原因異常潮濕，有些地方甚至被苔蘚覆蓋。 壁面中段有為數不多的幾塊刻經倖存下來。窟頂下方近70釐米高的一條水平區域，可以說是e壁保存最好的一段壁面。

row.cell: ［……（缺20字）］塔廟寶像……；繼續d壁的《法華經》卷一第二品《方便品》。
［妙法蓮華經卷第一］；現已不可見，依1號窟f壁第48行補出。 表示卷一結束，亦即《法華經》卷一第二品《方便品》結束。
［妙法蓮華］經譬喻品［第三］；其後有一較小的“二”字，表示為《法華經》卷二第三品《譬喻品》之開始， 同時也是第二卷的開始。
妙法蓮華經［信解品第四］；表示《法華經》卷二第四品《信解品》的開始。缺失的字依2號窟e壁第20行補出。
一［乘］道隨宜；《法華經》卷二第四品《信解品》結尾部份最後可見的文字。
［妙法蓮華經卷第二］；現已不可見，據1號窟f壁第48行補出。 表示經文第二卷至此結束，同時也是《法華經》卷二第四品《信解品》的結束。
［妙法蓮華經藥草喻品第五］；現已不可見，依2號窟e壁第20行補出。 由1號窟f壁第48行的經品名寫刻情況推測，其後應有一較小的“三”字，表示第三卷的開始， 同時也是《法華經》卷三第五品《藥草喻品》的開始。
［妙法蓮華經］授記品［第六］；表示《法華經》卷三第六品《授記品》之開始。
住二十小劫其［佛］常處虛空為衆；《法華經》卷三第六品《授記品》可辨識出的最後幾個刻字，位置靠近該行頂端。
［無不愛樂］；現已不可見，據f壁開頭經文推測得出，e壁刻經至此結束，仍為《法華經》卷三第六品《授記品》中的內容。

list.item: 打磨面覆蓋整個窟壁，底端距現今水泥地面約10釐米。在靠近窟口的地方，壁面並非呈直角與窟頂相交，而是逐漸併入後者， 二者不存在一個明顯的分界線。

現存文字區的形狀大致是一個上緣不規則的矩形，面積約為222至232×184.5釐米。 該區自窟頂下方13至19釐米處始，止於現今地面上方31釐米處；距e壁12釐米，距窟口44釐米（上部）、17.5釐米（下部）。 第83行是有連續刻文的最後一行。其後第84到96行的字似乎起初被書丹，但後來只零星刻出一些。 第87行靠近頂端的地方有兩個單獨的“人”字，二字相隔12釐米，之間可填入七字， 可能為《妙法蓮華經》卷四第十品《法師品》中的“……知此人等，近於佛智慧。 若人說此……”。此外，第88行還有第三個“人”字。在文字區下部，距窟口17.5釐米、地面上方104釐米的地方， 有一塊大小約為20×15釐米的縱矩形區域，其內有較多的刻字，不過多風化難辨。 位於第96行的“問”字是此窟最後一個可辨識的刻字。

row.cell: 0.5至1.0釐米，大約在42行後行距縮小

div.head: 保存狀況
div.p: 保存不良。牆基表層遭侵蝕，高度達37釐米。e壁下部70至90釐米寬的剝蝕帶延伸至f壁。與對面的d壁一樣，f壁也有一塊三角形區域的壁面表層幾乎全部剝落。該區域自窟口始，範圍為地面上方140至210釐米之間， 後逐漸變窄，向窟內延伸130釐米後停止。小塊的剝落接著向後壁e方向繼續剝落。 地面上方約195至230釐米之間，是一條風化剝蝕較嚴重的水平帶。
刻字區下部朝向窟口方向的延伸部份，僅於地面之上110至125釐米之間可見。
壁面後部，窟頂下方有一道細裂縫，它在刻經前就已經存在了。在第1、7、13、14行，刻經者考慮到了這條裂縫，經文跳過它繼續。 該裂隙之後又繼續擴大，破壞了第2至6行頂部的部份刻字。但它並沒有影響到第8至16行（第12行除外）經文。 到第17行時，裂縫不再經過文字區。

row.cell: 佛於其中度無量眾其佛法中……；e壁《法華經》卷三第六品《授記品》之繼續。
汝等善聽；《法華經》卷三第六品《授記品》最後幾字。
妙法蓮［華經化］城喻品第七；表示《法華經》卷三第七品《化城喻品》自此開始。
妙法蓮［華］經卷第三；表示卷三結束，同時亦是《法華經》第七品《化城喻品》之結束。
［妙法蓮華］經五百弟子受記品第八；其後有一小字“四”； 表示《法華經》第八品《五百弟子受記品》之開始， 同時也是第四卷經文的開始。
槃分，自足不求餘。今佛覺悟我，言非實滅……；《法華經》卷四第八品《五百弟子受記品》結尾部份的最後可見經文。
［妙法蓮華經授學無學人記品第九 爾時］阿難，羅睺羅而作是念……；缺失文字據2號窟e壁第20行補出。表示《法華經》卷四第九品《授學無學人記品》之開始。
聞普周遍，漸入於涅槃。爾時［學、無學二千人，聞佛授記，歡喜踊躍］，而說偈言［世尊慧燈明，我聞授記音，心歡喜充滿， 如甘露見灌］；為《法華經》卷四第九品《授學無學人記品》的結束部份。
［妙法蓮華經法師品第十］；現已不可見；泯滅字跡據2號窟e壁第20行補出。表示《法華經》卷四第十品《法師品》由此開始。
諸天、龍王、夜叉、乾闥婆……；《法華經》卷四第十品《法師品》開頭可辨識出的刻字。
［信解］受持者當知是人得近阿耨多……；f壁最後一行完整行的開頭，屬《法華經》卷四第十品《法師品》。 前一行與之高度大致相同的刻字為：……者何此中已有如來全身……。
［妙法蓮華經見寶塔品第十一］；打磨面上並未刻字，依2號窟e壁第20行補出。《法華經》卷四第十一品《見寶塔品》應自此始。
［……］上四面皆出［……］；該行可辨識出的五字，屬《法華經》卷四第十一品《見寶塔品》。
［……］哉釋迦［……］；該行可辨識出的三字，屬《法華經》卷四第十一品《見寶塔品》。
［……］緣有此［……］；該行可辨識出的三字，屬《法華經》卷四第十一品《見寶塔品》。
［……］諸佛［……］；該行可辨識出的二字，屬《法華經》卷四第十一品《見寶塔品》。
［……］問［……］；該行可辨識出的一字，仍屬《法華經》卷四第十一品《見寶塔品》。其後尚有足夠的壁面空間完成第十一品。

div.head: Overview: Caves 1 and 2
div.p: The two caves, 1 and 2, form one of the three pairs of twin caves in the precinct. The other two pairs are caves 83 with 85, and caves 109 with 110. Twin caves are positioned side by side and separated by a common wall. They have the same shape and size, and their floors are on the same level. All three pairs happen to be high up on their respective cliffs.

The twin caves 83 and 85 contain a part of the Great Parinirvāṇa Sutra (Foshuo da boniepan jing 佛說大般涅槃經) in 83, and the entire Sutra Spoken by Vimalakīrti (Weimojie suoshuo jing 維摩詰所說經) in 85. Caves 109 and 110 contain a single text, the Great Skillful Means Sutra on the Buddha’s Repayment of Kindness (Dafangbian fo baoen jing 大方便佛報恩經). However, only five of the seven scrolls of the sutra have been carved, and only five of the six walls in these twin caves have been used. The twin caves, 1 and 2, are the most accomplished pair: they contain a single text in its entirety, and all of their six walls are filled with it. This text is the Lotus Sutra of the Wonderful Law (Miaofa lianhua jing 妙法蓮華經).

The makers decided to carve the most popular version of the Sutra, the one translated by Kumārajīva (334–413), with 28 chapters in seven scrolls. This text has around 71,000 characters, that is, almost one fifth of the entire volume of text still extant in the Grove of the Reclining Buddha.

Standard sized caves, whose walls could accommodate ca. ten thousand characters, would not have provided enough wall space for the complete sutra. The makers opted, therefore, for a standard size cave with extended height. As discussed in the introduction, the ground plan of such caves is the same as that of standard size caves. In cave 1 the entrance opening has a width of 211 cm, and in 2 of 206 cm. The three walls, too, are not much wider than the usual two meters. What is different, however, is the height. Increasing the height added space on the three interior walls, and helped the builders optimally to reconcile their two goals: maximizing the wall surface and minimizing the volume of cut out rock. Wall d in cave 1 is 275 cm high at the entrance, and wall f 282 cm. The respective measurements in cave 2 are only slightly smaller, 265 cm and 260 cm. When carving this second cave, the makers may have worked out by then that they did not need the full height allowed in cave 1 to complete the sutra.

As the calligraphers began work on wall d in cave 1, they laid out a perfect field of script, with a frame and a grid of vertical and horizontal lines, giving them columns and rows of squares of absolutely equal size, each measuring 2.1 cm × 2.1 cm. They also made the best use of the available space. At the entrance, their field of script is only 1 cm less wide than the polished area. On wall e, the calligraphers first continued in the same way, but after column 75, they abandoned the horizontal rows. With this irregularity, they could fit more characters into a column. In the last 17 columns, they also tightened the width between them. The calligraphers may have been afraid that the available wall space would not be sufficient, if they had continued in the same rhythm as before. Similarly, they tightened the width between their columns after column 48 on wall f. Thus, in cave 1, they managed to complete the sutra with the full chapter 11, entitled Jian baota pin 見寶塔品, in scroll 4.

In the neighboring cave 2, the calligraphers continued with chapter 12 of the Sutra. Here they did away with grid lines and a rectangular frame for the field of script. Beginning from column 55 on wall d, the columns become higher by steps adding up to ca. 6 cm, which created more space for characters. The carvers were so concerned to save space that they carved the title of the new chapter 17 into the lowest part of the last column on this wall d; then, on wall e, they immediately began with the text. On this wall, the calligraphers gained further surface space, because the wall is slightly concave, and because the ground plan of the cave also widens slightly toward the rear. While the entrance opening is 206 cm wide, wall e has a width of 221 cm. Having been so parsimonious with the available space, the calligraphers must have realized that, on the last wall f, they could be more generous. They introduced vertical grid lines again, with a distance of 2.0-2.1 cm between them, but they did not create horizontal rows. The calligraphers only required 71 columns on this last wall to complete the entire sutra, at a comfortable distance of 42 cm before the entrance opening. The polished area continues to the end. Had the calligraphers so wished, the wall would have provided enough space for the postface (houxu 後序) of the Sutra,T#263, 9: 62b-c. and even for a colophon, as in cave 66.

The constant changes in the layout of the columns on the six walls of caves 1 and 2 suggest that the calligraphers could not, or did not, compute perfectly how much wall space they would ultimately need. Their modifications reflect their estimates of the remaining work as they went along. It is also conceivable that, at those points, other designers and calligraphers took over.

The calligraphers were methodical in their use of text markers to indicate the beginnings and ends of the 7 scrolls and 28 chapters of the Sutra. They marked the beginning of each scroll with its respective number in a small character. The small characters 2, 4, 5, and 6 are still visible. The others have been supplied here by analogy with the small number 4, which marks the beginning of scroll 4 in cave 1, wall f, column 48, and the small number 6, which marks the beginning of scroll 6 in cave 2, wall e, column 20.

figure.head: 1、2號窟二窟平面圖；雷魯卡繪製
Ground plans of caves 1 and 2; drawing Lukas Ledderose

div.p: The end of a scroll, the calligraphers marked by the sutra title, together with the scroll number, as in: “Scroll number 3 in the Lotus Sutra 妙法蓮華經卷第三.” Only in the case of scrolls 3, 4, 6, and 7, are these markers still visible. This information has been supplied for the other three scrolls by analogy with the full text marking the end of scroll 3 in cave 1, wall f, column 48. At the beginning of a chapter, the calligraphers repeated the title of the sutra, followed by the title of the chapter (ending with pin 品), followed by the number of the chapter, as in: “Lotus Sutra, chapter entitled Suixi gongde pin, number 18 妙法蓮華經隨喜功德品第十八.” These markers are no longer visible for chapters 1, 5, 9, 10, 11, 12, 14, 21, and 23. They have been supplied here by analogy with the beginning of chapter 18 in cave 2, wall e, column 20. Unlike the scrolls, the end of a chapter is never indicated.

div.head: Location

list.item: The westernmost cave in the northern cliff, section G; floor level ca. 7 m above the present walkway at the foot of the cliff (fig. and fig. ).
Direction of wall f: 148˚.

div.p: The positions of caves 1 and 2 are among the highest in the precinct, but in the Tang dynasty, they would have been yet higher by a few meters. The width of the facade between the openings of the twin caves is 135 cm.

Evidently, a site close to the colossal sculpture of the reclining Buddha was the most desirable position for the caves. Their floors are at the same height as the top of the head of the guardian figure standing at the Buddha’s feet about 9 m away to the east from wall d of cave 2.

div.head: Shape
div.p: Box-shaped with open front and double entrance frame (fig. ). Like the other two pairs of twin caves, caves 1 and 2 share the outer entrance frame (fig. ). As in all caves in the precinct, the outer frame is less accurately cut than the inner entrance frame. It includes a short common roof, which in cave 1 protrudes ca. 40 cm beyond the inner frame. At the entrance opening, about 45 cm below the present concrete floor, the outer frame formed a narrow walkway between the two caves; only traces remain.

In the face of the eastern reveal, which runs parallel to wall d, a rectangular hole, 15 × 11 cm, has been cut 16 cm below the lintel and 38 cm from the inner frame. Its state of preservation is good; only the contours are weathered. A corresponding hole, 15 × 15 cm, remains in the reveal of wall f, 13 cm below the lintel and 33 cm from the inner frame. It is heavily worn and is now only 2-4 cm deep. These two holes may have held a horizontal bar serving as the upper frame of a door, or holding a curtain that was hung from it. No traces of such corresponding holes are visible in the reveal of cave 2.
The cave has an approximately square ground plan, widening toward the rear; the ceiling descends slightly toward the rear.

list.item: Wall d: height at front 275 cm, at rear 262 cm, width 205 cm.
Wall e: height at midpoint 268 cm, width 254 cm.
Wall f: height at front 282 cm, at rear 266 cm, width 224 cm.

div.head: State of preservation
div.p: In 1982 a protective chemical coating 化學封護處理保護 was applied to the surfaces. 38b. A section of the wall between caves 1 and 2 has fallen out and has been replaced by concrete. The dilapidated door frame has been stabilized by concrete on its three sides, including the lintel. The original floor was ca. 10 cm higher than the present one, which is now covered in concrete. The front part of the ceiling is worn and displays a yellow color. Flat slabs of the rock have fallen from the rear part. Also at the rear, earth has accumulated on which there is now vegetation.

list.item: A polished area, ca. 240 × 183 cm, begins ca. 23 cm from the entrance and immediately below the ceiling; it covers the entire wall d, going all the way to wall e and ends ca. 10 cm above the present floor.
The field of script, ca. 205 × 168 cm, begins ca. 24 cm from the entrance and 20 cm below the ceiling. It ends ca. 25 cm above the present floor and 13-16 cm from wall e.

div.head: State of preservation
div.p: Poor. The surface has flaked away to a depth of 2-3 cm over a large angular brownish colored area, beginning at a height of 208 cm at the entrance, and extending to a point at 106 cm into the cave. More areas have flaked below this section. A horizontal zone of the wall, which reaches ca. 65 cm down from the ceiling, and a vertical zone in the rear, which begins 162 cm from the entrance, are most fully preserved.

row.cell: ［妙法蓮華經序品第一］, no longer visible;The two characters “序品” are still visible on the rubbing made in the 1980s. See . missing characters supplied by analogy with cave 2, wall e, column 20. Assumed to have been followed by a small character 1, yi 一, by analogy with cave 1, wall e, column 13, and cave 1, wall f, column 48; no longer visible. Refers to the beginning of chapter 1, entitled Xu pin 序品 (preface), coinciding with the beginning of scroll 1 of the Lotus Sutra .
.../[...]我聞一時佛...; the first visible characters in chapter 1, entitled Xu pin 序品 (preface), in scroll 1 of the Lotus Sutra.
...說《法華經》今相如本瑞是諸[...] 盡無有餘; the last visible characters in chapter 1, in scroll 1 of the Lotus Sutra.
妙［法蓮華經方便品第二 爾時］世尊從三昧...; missing characters supplied by analogy with cave 2, wall e, column 20. Refers to the beginning of chapter 2, entitled Fangbian pin 方便品, in scroll 1 of the Lotus Sutra.
...爪甲,而畫作佛像［如是］諸人［等，漸］漸［積］功［德，具足大］; the last characters on wall d, in chapter 2, entitled Fangbian pin 方便品, in scroll 1 of the Lotus Sutra.

list.item: A polished area, ca. 254 × 258 cm, covers the entire wall, ending ca. 10 cm above the present floor.
The field of script begins 13 cm from wall d and ca. 21 cm below the ceiling. It ends ca. 12 cm from wall f. The lower edge is no longer visible.

row.cell: only in the first part; they become irregular after column 75
0.5 to 1.0 cm, tighter in the last 17 columns: 0.3 to 0.7 cm
ca. 0.5 to 1.2 cm, tighter in the last 17 columns: 0.1 to 0.3 cm

div.head: State of preservation
div.p: Poor. The entire lower part has disappeared, up to a height of ca. 86 cm, where a moist zone, partly covered with moss, runs across the entire width of the wall. A zone, approximately 70 cm high, below the ceiling is the best preserved section. Several patches of characters still survive in the central zone.

row.cell: [...20 characters] 塔廟寶像; is the continuation from wall d, of chapter 2, entitled Fangbian pin 方便品, in scroll 1 of the Lotus Sutra.

［妙法蓮華經卷第一］; no longer visible; missing characters supplied by analogy with cave 1, wall f, column 48. Refers to the end of scroll 1, coinciding with the end of chapter 2, entitled Fangbian pin 方便品, in scroll 1 of the Lotus Sutra.

［妙法蓮華］經譬喻品［第三］; followed by a small character 2, er 二; refers to the beginning of chapter 3, entitled Piyu pin 譬喻品, in scroll 2 of the Lotus Sutra; coinciding with the beginning of scroll 2 of the Lotus Sutra.
妙法蓮華經［信解品第四］; refers to the beginning of chapter 4, entitled Xinjie pin 信解品, in scroll 2 of the Lotus Sutra. Missing characters supplied by analogy with cave 2, wall e, column 20.
一［乘］道隨宜; the last visible characters at the end of chapter 4, entitled Xinjie pin 信解品, in scroll 2 of the Lotus Sutra.

［妙法蓮華經卷第二］; no longer visible; missing characters supplied by analogy with cave 1, wall f, column 48. Refers to the end of scroll 2, coinciding with the end of chapter 4, entitled Xinjie pin 信解品, in scroll 2 of the Lotus sutra.

［妙法蓮華經藥草喻品第五］ no longer visible; missing characters supplied by analogy with cave 2, wall e, column 20. Assumed to have been followed by a small character 3, san
三, by analogy with cave 1, wall f, column 48. Refers to the beginning of scroll 3, coinciding with the beginning of chapter 5, entitled Yaocaoyu pin 藥草喻品, in scroll 3 of the Lotus Sutra.
［妙法蓮華經］授記品［第六］; refers to the beginning of chapter 6, entitled Shouji pin 授記品, in scroll 3 of the Lotus Sutra.
住二十小劫其［佛］常處虛空為衆; last visible characters near the top of the column, in chapter 6, entitled Shouji pin 授記品, in scroll 3 of the Lotus Sutra.
［無不愛樂］; no longer visible; according to the beginning text of wall f, it should be reconstructed as: last characters on wall e, in chapter 6, entitled Shouji pin 授記品, in scroll 3 of the Lotus Sutra.

list.item: A polished area extends over the entire wall down to about ca. 10 cm above the present floor. Near the entrance, the upper edge of the wall does not meet the ceiling at a right angle, but gradually merges into it.

The present field of script, a rectangle, irregular at the top, ca. 222-232 × 184.5 cm, begins 12 cm from wall e and 13-19 cm below the ceiling, and it ends ca. 31 cm above the present floor. The distance to the entrance in its upper part is 44 cm, in its lower part 17.5 cm. Column 83 is the last column with a continuous string of characters. From columns 84-96, characters were apparently originally written by the brush, but only carved sporadically. Two single characters ren 人 in column 87 are separated by 12 cm, which may have been filled by 7 characters. Those may have been part of the passage “...知此人等, 近於佛智慧. 若人說此...” in chapter 10, entitled Fashi pin 法師品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra. A third character ren 人 is seen in column 88. In the lower part of the field of script, 17.5 cm from the entrance and 104 cm above the floor, a vertical rectangle, 20 × 15 cm, contains traces of more characters. One character, wen 問, in column 96 is the last recognizable character in this cave.

div.head: State of preservation
div.p: Poor. The surface of the base of the wall is eroded up to a height of 37 cm. The eroded zone of a width between 70-90 cm on wall e extends into wall f. As on the opposite wall d, the surface has almost completely disappeared across a triangular field, beginning at the entrance at a height from 140-210 cm and leading to a point ca. 130 cm into the cave. Flaked patches continue toward the rear wall e. More flaked areas occur in a horizontal zone at a height between 195 and 230 cm.
The lower extension of the field of script toward the entrance is only visible between 110 and 125 cm above the floor.

Before engraving began, a thin horizontal crack was already present beneath the ceiling in the rear part of wall f. In columns 1, 7, 13, and 14, the carver took this crack into account by skipping over it. The crack later widened and damaged characters in columns 2-6. It did not affect characters from columns 8-16, except for column 12. In column 17, the crack leaves the field of script.

row.cell: 佛於其中度無量眾其佛法中...; is the continuation from wall e of chapter 6, entitled Shouji pin 授記品, in scroll 3 of the Lotus Sutra.
汝等善聽; the last characters in chapter 6, entitled Shouji pin 授記品, in scroll 3 of the Lotus Sutra.
妙法蓮［華經化］城喻品第七; refers to the beginning of chapter 7, entitled Huachengyu pin 化城喻品, in scroll 3 of the Lotus Sutra.
妙法蓮［華］經卷第三; refers to the end of scroll 3, coinciding with the end of chapter 7, entitled Huachengyu pin 化城喻品, in the Lotus Sutra.
［妙法蓮華］經五百弟子受記品第八; followed by a small character 4, si 四; refers to the beginning of chapter 8, entitled Wubai dizi shouji pin 五百弟子受記品, coinciding with the beginning of scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.
槃分，自足不求餘。今佛覺悟我，言非實滅...; the last visible characters at the end of chapter 8, entitled Wubai dizi shouji pin 五百弟子受記品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.

［妙法蓮華經授學無學人記品第九。爾時］阿難，羅睺羅而作是念...; missing characters supplied by analogy with cave 2, wall e, column 20. Refers to the beginning of chapter 9, entitled Shouxue wuxue ren ji pin 授學無學人記品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.
聞普周遍，漸入於涅槃。爾時［學、無學二千人，聞佛授記，歡喜踊躍，］而說偈言［世尊慧燈明，我聞授記音， 心歡喜充滿，如甘露見灌］; the end of chapter 9, entitled Shouxue wuxue ren ji pin 授學無學人記品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.
［妙法蓮華經法師品第十］; no longer visible; missing characters supplied by analogy with cave 2, wall e, column 20. Refers to the beginning of chapter 10, entitled Fashi pin 法師品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.
諸天、龍王、夜叉、乾闥婆...; the first visible characters in chapter 10, entitled Fashi pin 法師品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.

［信解］受持者當知是人得近阿耨多...; the beginning of the last complete column on wall f; characters in chapter 10, entitled Fashi pin 法師品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra. Characters at the same height in the preceding column: ...者何此中已有如來全身...

［妙法蓮華經見寶塔品第十一］; the polished area was never engraved; missing characters supplied by analogy with cave 2, wall e, column 20. Refers to the beginning of chapter 11, entitled Jian baota pin 見寶塔品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.
[...]上四面皆出[...]; discernible characters from chapter 11, entitled Jian baota pin 見寶塔品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.
[...]哉釋迦[...]; discernible characters from chapter 11, entitled Jian baota pin 見寶塔品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.
[...]緣有此[...]; discernible characters from chapter 11, entitled Jian baota pin 見寶塔品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.
[...]諸佛[...]; discernible characters from chapter 11, entitled Jian baota pin 見寶塔品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra.
[...]問[...]; discernible character from chapter 11, entitled Jian baota pin 見寶塔品, in scroll 4 of the Lotus Sutra. Enough space was left on wall f to complete chapter 11.

note.p: Hu and Li are the first to identify the text in the two caves as the Lotus Sutra in Kumārajīva’s translation, and give the titles of five chapters (15, 18, 23, 24, and 25).

Peng gives measurements of the three dimensions of the caves and computes their volume as 13.29 m³ (cave 1) and 12.836 m³ (cave 2). He identifies the Lotus Sutra as the only text carved in the two caves and the titles of five (one of them twice) chapters, locating them on the walls. He describes the state of preservation on all six walls as “rather good 較好”.
Cao gives the location of some chapters on the walls.

Hu gives measurements of the three dimensions of both caves (on the same lines as 6), but does not compute their volume. He identifies chapter 8, 五百弟子受記品, on wall f, cave 1, locates chapter 15 on wall d, cave 2, chapter 18 on wall e, and chapters 23, 24, 25 on wall f. He identifies the version of the Lotus Sutra as the translation by Kumārajīva.

Li and Deng believe that the two caves were carved in the Tang dynasty. They give location, shape, and measurements of the caves. They also set out the measurements of the fields of script, and state the total number of characters in cave 1 as 9,170 (wall d), 12,407 (wall e), 9,317 (wall f), and the total number of characters in cave 2 as 7,797 (wall d), 9,709 (wall e), 9,072 (wall f). They report that the characters have a diameter of 1.5-2.0 cm.
They describe the state of preservation of the sutra in both caves as “rather good 較好”, and mention that in 1982 a protective chemical coating 化學封護處理保護 was applied to the surfaces of walls.
This report gives a detailed description of the location and shape of the two caves. It is suggested that the twin sutra caves were part of an overall unified plan.

Cave 1 was inaccessible at the time of the field work, because a storehouse had been built below it. Thus this report provides measurements only for cave 2 and its fields of script, and makes observations on the state of preservation: Wall d, lower part already decayed; middle part slightly damaged; upper part well preserved. Wall e, lower part completely eroded; sutras only preserved on the upper part. Wall f: middle part near the entrance severely eroded; upper part rather well preserved. Also mentioned are two holes in wall d, which were added later. The floor is ca. 10 cm lower than the original level.

The report identifies the Lotus Sutra as the only text in two caves and states that it was carved in its entirety. It locates scrolls and chapters in cave 1 based on rubbings in the Anyue Cultural Relics Bureau and on the walls of cave 2 based on on-site inspection.

Lee publishes her photo of caves 1 and 2 (p. 67, fig. 24) and a drawing (p. 48, fig. 14) with a hypothetical reconstruction of the wooden building for the statue of the reclining Buddha and its relation to the two caves. She believes that the two caves were carved in the first half of the eighth century.

In her view, the Lotus Sutra in Kumārajīva’s translation, which is presented in full and situated beside the colossal image, provides an authoritative case for the use of material objects in the devotional practice and worship of the Buddha.

She claims that the Lotus Sutra can be regarded “as the generative source of Wofoyuan, not unlike the role of the reclining Buddha in defining the parameters of a ‘situation’ to which a ‘response’ was made.” The colossal figure and the sutra bracket the time frame after the Buddha’s entering nirvana, and rely on the nirvana theme to call attention to the survival of Buddhism in an age without the Buddha’s presence. The other Buddhist stone sutras on the opposite side of the valley offer a response that seeks to rationalize and uphold the permanence of the Buddhist faith.
Cao and Yu publish rubbings (nos. 0009-0011, 0046-0048) of parts of the Lotus Sutra engraved in two caves.

They give measurements of the fields of script and the number of the characters still extant on each wall in caves 1 and 2 as follows: Cave 1: wall d 1,315 characters extant; wall e 751 characters extant; wall f 3,001 characters extant.
Cave 2: wall d 7,308 characters extant; wall e 3,906 characters extant; wall f 3,529 characters extant.

The authors give a brief introduction to the Lotus Sutra and its translator Kumārajīva. They also list titles of scrolls and chapters of the sutra. They believe that the Lotus Sutra at Wofoyuan was not engraved in its entirety, and that the faithful, who donated money, determined which scrolls or chapters were engraved.
The two caves are situated to the west of the feet of the reclining Buddha. As this is a site in the cliff face that is less desirable, they must have been carved after the colossal statue.
